agent_output:
# Domain Analysis — Delivery ETA Display Change

## Domain Analysis

**Business Context:** The system currently communicates delivery ETAs to customers as precise timestamps (e.g., "Delivery by 14:30 on Friday"). The proposal is to reduce precision to date-only windows (e.g., "Delivery by Friday"). This changes a core customer-facing promise — the delivery commitment — which is one of the most critical touchpoints in the logistics domain.

**Ubiquitous Language:**

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **ETA (Estimated Time of Arrival)** | The system's prediction of when a package will be delivered to the recipient. This is a *promise* to the customer, not just a data point. |
| **Delivery Window** | The time range within which delivery is expected. Currently implicit (exact time ± some tolerance); proposed change makes it explicit (entire calendar day). |
| **Delivery Commitment** | The contractual or implied promise made to the customer about when their package arrives. Drives SLA enforcement, refund eligibility, and customer trust. |
| **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** | The formal or informal agreement on delivery performance. Changing ETA granularity directly changes what constitutes an SLA breach. |
| **Last-Mile Delivery** | The final leg of delivery from distribution hub to recipient. Most variable, most expensive, and most visible to the customer. |
| **On-Time Delivery Rate (OTD)** | KPI measuring percentage of deliveries that arrive within the committed window. Changing window size mechanically changes this metric. |

**Bounded Context:** Customer Communication / Order Tracking (intersects with Routing, SLA Enforcement, and Customer Support)

---

## Business Rules

| # | Rule | Invariant? | Testable Assertion |
|---|------|------------|-------------------|
| 1 | A delivery commitment, once communicated to a customer, defines the SLA for that shipment | **Yes** | Given a customer has been shown an ETA, when the delivery occurs after that ETA, then the system must flag it as a late delivery |
| 2 | ETA precision must match or exceed the precision of any SLA-linked refund or penalty trigger | **Yes** | Given refund policies reference time-of-day (e.g., "if not delivered by end of business"), when ETA is date-only, then refund trigger logic must also be date-only |
| 3 | ETA changes must be communicated proactively to the customer before the original window expires | **Yes** | Given an ETA has been communicated, when the system detects delivery will miss that window, then a notification must be sent before the original ETA passes |
| 4 | Premium/express service tiers must communicate ETAs at higher precision than standard tiers | No (soft) | Given a customer has paid for express/same-day service, when ETA is displayed, then it should include time-of-day precision |
| 5 | Internal routing and dispatch systems must retain full timestamp precision regardless of customer-facing display | **Yes** | Given ETAs are displayed as date-only to customers, when internal systems process the same shipment, then full datetime precision must be preserved |
| 6 | Historical OTD metrics must not be retroactively inflated by changing the measurement window | **Yes** | Given historical deliveries were measured against timestamp ETAs, when reporting includes post-change deliveries, then the two measurement regimes must be clearly separated |

---

## Business Impact

### Financial

**Negative risks:**
- **Premium service erosion.** Customers paying for express/priority service expect time-precision. Removing it devalues the premium tier, potentially reducing willingness to pay surcharges. Express delivery premiums are typically 30-80% above standard rates — this revenue is at risk if the perceived value drops.
- **Competitive disadvantage.** Major competitors (Amazon, FedEx, UPS) trend toward *more* precise windows (2-hour, 1-hour). Moving to less precision is counter-market.
- **Hidden refund savings are a trap.** Yes, a wider window means fewer "late" deliveries and fewer refund triggers. But this is an accounting trick, not a service improvement. If regulators or partners audit, it looks like gaming the metric.

**Potential upside:**
- Reduced refund/credit payouts in the short term due to wider compliance window.
- Lower customer support volume for "where is my package arriving at 14:30 and it's 14:35" type calls.

### Operational

- **Positive:** Dispatchers and route optimizers gain flexibility. A date-only commitment means loads can be resequenced without triggering SLA breaches, improving route efficiency by an estimated 5-15%.
- **Positive:** Fewer ETA-update notifications to send when minor delays occur (stuck in traffic 20 min ≠ missed window anymore).
- **Negative:** Internal systems MUST still track full precision. This is NOT a simplification of the data model — it's a *display layer* change only. If anyone treats this as license to reduce internal precision, routing quality will degrade.
- **Negative:** Customer support agents lose a key tool. When a customer asks "when today?", the agent either has the data (and withholds it, which feels dishonest) or doesn't have it (which is a regression).

### User Impact

- **Negative — this is the critical dimension.** Customers overwhelmingly prefer more precision, not less. Industry research consistently shows that delivery time visibility is a top-3 driver of customer satisfaction in e-commerce logistics.
- **"Delivery by Friday" forces the customer to be home all day** or risk missing the delivery. This is a material quality-of-life regression, especially for residential deliveries requiring signature.
- **Trust erosion.** If the system *has* the data but chooses not to show it, customers will perceive this as the company hedging — hiding behind vague windows because they can't reliably hit precise ones. This is worse than showing a precise ETA and occasionally missing it.
- **B2B customers** (businesses receiving shipments) need time-of-day precision for warehouse staffing and dock scheduling. Date-only windows are operationally unacceptable for this segment.

### Risk

- **Compliance risk:** Some regulated industries (pharmaceuticals, perishables) require documented delivery windows with time precision. Date-only may violate contractual obligations.
- **Metric gaming risk:** On-time delivery rate will mechanically improve without any actual service improvement. This creates a false signal that may mask real operational problems. Leadership decisions based on inflated OTD are dangerous.
- **Competitive risk:** This moves against the market trend. Customers accustomed to precise windows from other carriers will view this as a downgrade.
- **Rollback risk:** Once customer expectations are reset to date-only, re-introducing time precision later requires re-earning trust. The damage is asymmetric — easy to remove, hard to restore.

### Stakeholders Affected

| Stakeholder Group | Impact |
|-------------------|--------|
| **End customers (residential)** | Must plan around full-day windows instead of specific times. Negative experience, especially for signature-required deliveries. |
| **End customers (B2B)** | Severe impact — warehouse/dock scheduling requires time-of-day precision. May violate receiving agreements. |
| **Customer support** | Increased "when exactly?" calls. Agents will either withhold data they have (trust issue) or genuinely lack data (capability issue). |
| **Sales / Account Management** | Harder to sell premium tiers if ETA precision doesn't differentiate. Enterprise clients may push back or seek competitors. |
| **Operations / Dispatch** | Gains flexibility in route optimization. This is the primary beneficiary. |
| **Product / Leadership** | OTD metrics will appear to improve, creating false confidence. Must be briefed on metric discontinuity. |
| **Finance** | Short-term refund reduction; medium-term revenue risk from premium tier devaluation and churn. |
| **Compliance / Legal** | Must audit all contractual obligations for time-precision requirements before rollout. |

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### If this change proceeds, these MUST hold:

| # | Criterion | Given | When | Then |
|---|-----------|-------|------|------|
| 1 | Internal precision preserved | Internal systems track delivery ETA as full datetime | ETA is displayed to customer | Only the date component is shown; full precision retained internally |
| 2 | Premium tiers exempt | Customer has paid for express/same-day/priority service | ETA is displayed | Time-of-day precision is still shown (not date-only) |
| 3 | B2B accounts exempt | Recipient is a business account with dock scheduling | ETA is displayed | Time-of-day precision is still shown |
| 4 | SLA alignment | Refund/penalty policies reference time-of-day thresholds | Date-only ETA is in effect | Refund triggers are updated to use end-of-day (23:59) as threshold |
| 5 | Metric discontinuity flagged | OTD reporting includes deliveries before and after the change | Reports are generated | Pre-change and post-change OTD are reported separately with clear labels |
| 6 | Customer opt-in to precision | A customer requests more precise ETA via tracking page or app | System has internal precision available | System shows time-of-day estimate (even if not the default display) |
| 7 | Support agent visibility | A support agent looks up a shipment | Shipment has full datetime ETA internally | Agent can see and communicate the precise ETA to the customer |

### Edge Cases

| Scenario | Expected Behavior |
|----------|-------------------|
| Same-day delivery shown as date-only | Nonsensical — "Delivery by today" with no time is useless. Same-day MUST show time. |
| Cross-timezone delivery (sender in EST, recipient in PST) | "Delivery by Friday" — which Friday? Must use recipient's local timezone, and for date-boundary cases, this matters enormously. |
| Multi-package order spanning multiple days | Each package shows its own date. Do NOT collapse to the latest date — customer needs to know which items arrive when. |
| Delivery date is today and it's already 8 PM | Showing "Delivery by today" at 8 PM without time precision creates anxiety. Must either show time or show "Delivery by tomorrow" if unlikely today. |
| Perishable/temperature-sensitive shipment | Date-only is insufficient. Recipient must know time to ensure cold chain integrity. Must retain time precision. |

---

## Confidence Assessment

| Dimension | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| **Confidence Level** | **82%** |
| **High Confidence Areas** | User impact (negative), operational impact (mixed), premium tier risk, B2B incompatibility, metric gaming risk |
| **Low Confidence Areas** | Exact revenue impact of premium devaluation (need pricing data), contractual obligations for specific accounts (need legal review), current OTD rate and how much it would mechanically improve |
| **Assumptions** | (1) The system currently has reliable time-of-day precision internally and this is a display-only change. (2) Premium/express tiers exist and are priced at a surcharge. (3) B2B customers exist in the customer base. (4) Refund policies currently reference time-of-day thresholds. |

---

## Rejection Reasons

| # | Violation | Business Rule Broken |
|---|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Blanket removal of time precision across all service tiers** | Rule 4 — Premium/express tiers must communicate at higher precision. A customer paying for 2-hour delivery windows cannot be shown "Delivery by Friday." |
| 2 | **No exemption for B2B accounts** | Dock scheduling and warehouse staffing require time-of-day. Date-only breaks receiving workflows. |
| 3 | **OTD metric reported without discontinuity flag** | Rule 6 — Historical metrics must not be retroactively inflated. If the wider window mechanically improves OTD, this must be disclosed, not celebrated. |
| 4 | **Same-day delivery displayed as date-only** | Logically incoherent — "Delivery by today" is not actionable information. |

---

## Verdict

**REQUEST CHANGES.** The proposal as stated — a blanket switch from timestamp to date-only — is **not acceptable** in its current form. It violates business rules around premium tier differentiation, B2B requirements, and same-day delivery coherence.

**However**, a *nuanced* version could work:
- Default to date-only for **standard-tier residential** deliveries only
- Preserve time precision for **express, same-day, B2B, perishable, and signature-required** shipments
- Expose time precision on-demand (tracking page drill-down, support agent access)
- Flag OTD metric discontinuity in all reporting
- Audit contractual obligations before rollout

The operations team's desire for flexibility is legitimate. But the solution is to **decouple the internal routing flexibility from the customer-facing promise**, not to degrade the promise itself. You can optimize routes freely within a date window while still showing customers a time estimate — just set the time estimate conservatively enough that route changes rarely breach it.
